narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50 and a family from a high-income bracket, you've clearly put in significant effort to build a strong profile for law school. Your decision to pursue full-time studies also aligns with the path most students take, representing over 92% of applicants in our dataset. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 45.5%—a threshold that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to succeed. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's rank around the middle of the pack, signaling to the model that your later academic performance might not translate to bar success. Closely counterbalancing that was your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which is stronger than the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction toward passing by showing your capability in college-level work. Your LSAT score of 32, though lower than the dataset average of 36.7, still provided a moderate positive nudge toward success, while your high family income offered some additional reassurance compared to typical applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 2 had a smaller but negative influence, reinforcing concerns from your early school performance. Overall, these factors placed you below the profiles of students typically admitted, who tend to have higher decile rankings and stronger standardized test scores. While this decision is disappointing, it's not the end of your journey—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs. You might also explore part-time options or schools with more holistic reviews that weigh your high GPA and family support more heavily. We're rooting for you to turn this around and succeed in law; many do with targeted improvements. Keep pushing forward.
